label: delphi-r2-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Proposition to assess

"If the city awards the homelessness-services contract to Harbor Light Alliance while Okafor holds the board seat and her spouse holds her position — even with Okafor fully recused from the RFP and the award — the contract would be void or voidable under Government Code § 1090."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 67,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not correct: recusal probably does NOT cure the \u00a7 1090 problem here, because the facts (spouse's salary funded ~40% by city contracts, Harbor Light's ~60% historical win rate) point toward a direct/prohibited \"financial interest\" imputed to Okafor through her spouse's employment, not a merely \"remote interest\" under \u00a7 1091 that disclosure-and-abstention could validate. Under Thomson v. Call, a prohibited \u00a7 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body cannot be cured by that member's recusal from the vote \u2014 the taint runs to the whole body/contract, not just the individual's ballot. Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently qualify as a \u00a7 1091.5 \"noninterest\" (nonprofit-liaison exception), which would remove one prong of the problem, but that exception doesn't reach the separate and more serious spousal-salary interest. The main source of doubt is genuine ambiguity over whether the spousal employment interest is properly \"remote\" (fixed salary not tied to any one contract, arguably analogous to \u00a7 1091(b)(2)) rather than \"direct,\" which would flip the analysis and let a well-executed recusal-plus-disclosure procedure save the contract.",
  "rationales": [
    "Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 holds that a disqualifying \u00a7 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body is not cured by that member's abstention from the vote, because the statutory prohibition runs to the whole body, not just the individual's ballot.",
    "The spouse's ~$95,000 salary is community property (Fam. Code \u00a7 760) and California courts construe 'financially interested' under \u00a7 1090 broadly, resolving doubts in favor of disqualification (e.g., Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), supporting imputation of the spouse's interest to Okafor.",
    "The facts emphasize that Harbor Light's general operating budget (which funds the spouse's salary) is ~40% city-contract-derived and that Harbor Light wins ~60% of these awards, a close enough nexus between this specific $2.3M RFP and the spouse's job/compensation to plausibly push the interest out of \u00a7 1091's 'remote interest' category and into a direct, non-curable interest.",
    "Even if Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies as a \u00a7 1091.5 'noninterest' for noncompensated officers of an affiliated/liaison nonprofit, that exception is narrow and would not immunize the distinct spousal-employment financial interest, which is the more serious defect.",
    "A credible counterargument exists that the spouse's fixed salary (not contingent on any single contract's award) could instead be framed as a \u00a7 1091(b)-type remote interest curable by disclosure-plus-abstention, which is the main reason the probability is not higher than roughly two-thirds."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1090",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1091 (remote interests)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1091.5 (noninterests, incl. noncompensated nonprofit-officer/liaison provision \u2014 subsection/exact text needs verification)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1092 (voidness remedy)",
    "Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633",
    "Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565",
    "People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289",
    "Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201",
    "Family Code \u00a7 760 (community property)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether Okafor's (and her spouse's) interest correctly classifies as a \"remote interest\" under \u00a7 1091(b) \u2014 curable by disclosure and full abstention \u2014 versus a direct/prohibited financial interest to which Thomson v. Call's no-cure-by-recusal rule applies; this classification is outcome-determinative and I am not fully certain of the precise statutory text/scope of \u00a7 1091(b)'s employee-compensation carve-out or \u00a7 1091.5's nonprofit-liaison noninterest provision as applied to these specific facts."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 72.0, mean 73.0, std dev 4.6, range 67.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 68.0 and 78.0.

*(For the skew notes below, "lower-probability" = the three panelists at 67–70; "higher-probability" = the four panelists at 72–78.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**A. Recusal cannot cure a body member's § 1090 interest (Thomson v. Call / Stigall structural rule) — 7 of 7.** Every panelist invoked the rule that § 1090's prohibition runs to the contracting body as a whole, so an interested member's abstention/recusal from the vote does not itself validate the contract. No skew.

**B. Spouse's salary as an imputable disqualifying financial interest — 7 of 7.** All panelists treated the spouse's ~$95K salary as the central candidate interest. Within this theme, **5 panelists** (2 lower, 3 higher) explicitly grounded imputation in community-property law (Fam. Code §§ 751/760).

**C. Revenue-dependence nexus (40% city-contract funding / 60% historical win rate) — 6 of 7.** Six panelists (2 lower, 4 higher) cited these quantitative facts as pushing the spousal interest toward "direct/material" rather than remote or speculative. One lower-probability panelist did not rely on the percentages.

**D. Board seat likely separately excepted, but the exception does not reach the spousal salary — 6 of 7.** Six panelists reasoned that Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may fall within a statutory exception (most citing a § 1091.5 noninterest; one citing § 1091 remote-interest treatment), while emphasizing that any such exception would not cover the distinct spousal-employment interest. One lower-probability panelist took the opposite view on the board seat (see Disagreements, point 1).

**E. Acknowledged counterargument: fixed, non-contingent salary may be remote or too attenuated — 7 of 7.** Every panelist identified some version of this as the principal reason their probability was not higher. Panelists differ on where this doubt sits analytically (see Disagreements, points 2–3).

**F. No enumerated § 1091/1091.5 category squarely covers spousal employment with the contracting entity — 4 of 7** (1 lower, 3 higher; slight skew toward higher-probability panelists). These panelists asserted affirmatively that the exception catalog does not reach the spousal salary, rather than treating exception-fit as an open question.

**G. Strict/broad construction of "financially interested," resolving doubts toward disqualification — 4 of 7** (2 lower, 2 higher). Citing Stigall, Honig, and the statute's prophylactic purpose.

**H. Contrast with the Political Reform Act (recusal cures § 87100 but not § 1090) — 3 of 7 as a rationale** (all at or above the median; two additional panelists cited PRA provisions in authorities "for contrast" only). Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**I. Void/structural remedy under § 1092 (with disgorgement and § 1097 criminal exposure) as reinforcing that recusal is insufficient by design — 3 of 7**, all at 78. Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**J. Seniority of the spouse's role (Director of Programs, management/policy-adjacent) as analytically significant — 2 of 7**, both at 78. Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**K. Practical enforcement likelihood (losing-bidder or taxpayer standing under CCP § 526a) — 1 of 7.**

**L. Okafor's fiduciary duty to Harbor Light / dual-loyalty structural harm while shaping RFP criteria — 1 of 7.**

**M. Cure scenarios involving board resignation — 2 of 7**, reaching different conclusions (see Disagreements, point 4).

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Statutory classification of Okafor's own board seat.** Three incompatible readings appear: (a) most panelists treat the uncompensated board seat as a likely § 1091.5 "noninterest"; (b) one panelist classifies it as a § 1091 remote interest curable by disclosure-and-abstention; (c) one panelist argues it sits uneasily with the narrow § 1091.5(a) exception because it is a personal, governance-level interest carrying influence over the funded entity.

2. **Whether any § 1091(b) remote-interest category could reach the spousal salary at all.** Some panelists treat a spousal-employment/fixed-salary remote-interest route as a genuinely available path that could flip the outcome if properly invoked; others flatly state that no enumerated remote-interest or noninterest category covers a spouse's paid employment with the contracting nonprofit. These are incompatible readings of the exception catalog, not just different emphasis.

3. **Locus of the threshold doubt.** Panelists split on where the pivotal uncertainty lies: (a) whether the fixed, general-operating-budget salary is a § 1090 "financial interest" at all (with one panelist framing the alternative as a pure PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality question, where recusal would suffice), versus (b) assuming the interest exists and locating the uncertainty entirely in exception fit. These framings assign the same doubt to different analytical steps with different doctrinal consequences.

4. **What cure package (if any) short of the spouse leaving employment could work.** One panelist posits that board resignation + rigorous multi-stage recusal + confirmation of a genuine rank-and-file, non-contingent salary could make the contract lawful; another states that board resignation plus recusal likely would not cure the problem while the spouse remains employed on city-funded dollars.

5. **Whether the spouse's job title/seniority matters to exception fit.** Two panelists condition exception availability on the spouse being rank-and-file rather than a director-level employee; others analyze only the fixed-vs-contingent structure of compensation without treating role seniority as relevant.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by all 7 panelists:**
- Gov. Code § 1090
- Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests)
- Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests) — several panelists flagged the exact subsection/text as unverified
- Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633
- Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
- People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289

**Cited by 5:**
- Gov. Code § 1092 (voidness remedy)
- Gov. Code §§ 87100/87103 (Political Reform Act, mostly cited "for contrast")

**Cited by 4:**
- Fam. Code § 760 (community property)
- Fraser-Yamor v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 — three cited it as "Fraser-Yamor Agency"; one as "Fraser-Yamor Chevrolet" with a flag to verify the citation

**Cited by 3:**
- Gov. Code § 1097 (criminal penalties)
- Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050

**Cited by 2:**
- Unspecified California Attorney General opinions (one panelist referencing opinions on council-designated liaisons/board members; another referencing opinions on spousal employment with a contracting party; both flagged pinpoint cites as unverified)

**Cited by only 1:**
- Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114
- People v. Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2004) 32 Cal.4th 1223
- Code Civ. Proc. § 526a (taxpayer suits)
- Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222
- Fam. Code § 751

---

*Panelists: please review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above and submit your round-2 probability and any revised rationale. This summary organizes round-1 responses only; it does not endorse or weight any position.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":71,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Modest upward revision from my round-1 67 to 71. I remain convinced the core structural argument is sound: under Thomson v. Call, a disqualifying § 1090 interest held by a sitting body member is not cured by that member's own recusal, so if Okafor's community-property stake in her spouse's Harbor Light salary counts as a \"financial interest ... in the contract,\" the award is void/voidable regardless of how carefully she recuses. What moved me is the panel's near-unanimous view (Themes E/F) that no enumerated § 1091(b) remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest category cleanly covers a spouse's salaried, non-officer employment with the contracting nonprofit — meaning the \"fixed salary, non-contingent on this particular contract\" defense I leaned on in round 1 likely has no statutory home that would make it curable by disclosure-and-abstention even if accepted, and instead mostly bears on whether a \"financial interest\" exists at all (a threshold question, not a curability one). That reframing pushes outcomes toward \"interest exists and is uncurable by recusal\" more often than toward \"interest is remote and curable.\" I did not move as far as the panel median because I still credit a genuine, unresolved doctrinal gap: some authority and practical reasoning support treating highly attenuated, general-operating-budget-funded, non-contract-contingent employment interests as falling short of the \"financial interest\" threshold entirely (closer to a PRA-materiality question), which would mean § 1090 — and its no-cure-by-recusal rule — never engages in the first place. The proposition's binary framing (void or voidable) doesn't fully accommodate that possibility, so I keep meaningful weight on it.","rationales":["Thomson v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 is a genuinely close analogue (spousal/agency commission interest in a contracting party's dealings with the public body found disqualifying) — this supports imputation rather than treating the spousal interest as too remote to count.","The panel's convergence that no enumerated § 1091(b) remote-interest category or § 1091.5 noninterest provision squarely covers a spouse's salaried (non-officer, non-fixed-fee) employment with the contracting nonprofit is a genuinely new-to-me consideration: it means even a persuasive 'fixed, non-contingent salary' argument may lack a statutory mechanism to become curable-by-disclosure, and instead just feeds into a prior, separate question of whether § 1090 is triggered at all — this is what actually moved my number up.","I continue to withhold full deference to the panel median because a real doctrinal fork remains open and, in my view, under-resolved even in the summary: whether an interest this attenuated (general operating budget, only ~40% city-funded, spouse's compensation not contingent on any single award, Harbor Light's continued operation and her continued employment not plausibly turning on this one RFP) clears the 'financial interest ... in the contract' threshold under § 1090 at all, versus being properly analyzed only under PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality (where recusal is the established, sufficient cure) — this is a threshold classification question logically prior to, and distinct from, the remote-interest curability question the panel focused on.","Okafor's own uncompensated board seat plausibly falls within a § 1091.5-type noninterest for noncompensated nonprofit liaisons/officers (consistent with 6/7 panelists), which would remove one prong of the problem but does not reach the spousal-salary interest, which remains the dispositive and more serious defect driving my probability."],"authorities":["Gov. Code § 1090","Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests, incl. § 1091(b) enumerated categories — exact subsections need verification)","Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests, incl. noncompensated nonprofit-officer/liaison provision — exact subsection/text needs verification)","Gov. Code § 1092 (voidness remedy)","Gov. Code § 1097 (criminal penalties)","Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633","Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565","People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289","Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201","Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050","Family Code § 760 (community property)","Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103 (PRA, cited for contrast on recusal-as-cure)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the spousal-employment interest even clears § 1090's \"financial interest ... in the contract\" threshold in the first place, given its attenuation (general-operating-budget funding, only ~40% government-derived, salary not contingent on any single award) — versus assuming that threshold is met and asking only whether an exception applies. The panel largely analyzed this as an exception-fit question, but if a court instead resolves it as a threshold non-interest question (more akin to PRA materiality), § 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule may never engage, and the contract could survive on a well-executed recusal record alone. I cannot confidently resolve which framing a reviewing court would adopt on these facts.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"The consideration that actually moved me was Theme F/Disagreement 2: the panel's fairly strong showing (4/7 explicitly, more implicitly) that the enumerated § 1091(b)/§ 1091.5 catalog has no category that squarely fits a spouse's salaried, non-officer employment with a contracting nonprofit. In round 1 I treated 'fixed, non-contract-contingent salary' as a live remote-interest escape hatch under something like § 1091(b); the panel discussion made me realize that even if that factual characterization is correct, it may not map onto any actual statutory remote-interest category, so it wouldn't make the interest curable-by-disclosure — it would just bear on whether the interest exists at all. That reallocation of where the doubt sits pushed my number up modestly. I did not fully adopt the panel median because Disagreement 3 (threshold 'is this even a § 1090 interest, or a PRA-materiality-only question') persuades me there remains a genuine, separate escape route the panel didn't fully resolve either, and I'm not willing to treat majority head-count on a contested doctrinal classification as itself evidence of the correct legal answer — Delphi convergence here reflects shared professional priors more than a resolved authority, so I moved only partway rather than to the median/mean."}

data.position_summary: Modest upward revision from my round-1 67 to 71. I remain convinced the core structural argument is sound: under Thomson v. Call, a disqualifying § 1090 interest held by a sitting body member is not cured by that member's own recusal, so if Okafor's community-property stake in her spouse's Harbor Light salary counts as a "financial interest ... in the contract," the award is void/voidable regardless of how carefully she recuses. What moved me is the panel's near-unanimous view (Themes E/F) that no enumerated § 1091(b) remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest category cleanly covers a spouse's salaried, non-officer employment with the contracting nonprofit — meaning the "fixed salary, non-contingent on this particular contract" defense I leaned on in round 1 likely has no statutory home that would make it curable by disclosure-and-abstention even if accepted, and instead mostly bears on whether a "financial interest" exists at all (a threshold question, not a curability one). That reframing pushes outcomes toward "interest exists and is uncurable by recusal" more often than toward "interest is remote and curable." I did not move as far as the panel median because I still credit a genuine, unresolved doctrinal gap: some authority and practical reasoning support treating highly attenuated, general-operating-budget-funded, non-contract-contingent employment interests as falling short of the "financial interest" threshold entirely (closer to a PRA-materiality question), which would mean § 1090 — and its no-cure-by-recusal rule — never engages in the first place. The proposition's binary framing (void or voidable) doesn't fully accommodate that possibility, so I keep meaningful weight on it.
rationales: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633's structural rule — that § 1090's prohibition runs to the contracting body as a whole, so an interested member's recusal does not itself validate a contract the body makes — is essentially undisputed across the panel (7/7) and remains the load-bearing premise for the proposition; I give it high confidence.

Family Code § 760 community-property principles give Okafor a personal (not merely derivative) pecuniary stake in half her spouse's ~$95K salary, and Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 is a genuinely close analogue (spousal/agency commission interest in a contracting party's dealings with the public body found disqualifying) — this supports imputation rather than treating the spousal interest as too remote to count.

The panel's convergence that no enumerated § 1091(b) remote-interest category or § 1091.5 noninterest provision squarely covers a spouse's salaried (non-officer, non-fixed-fee) employment with the contracting nonprofit is a genuinely new-to-me consideration: it means even a persuasive 'fixed, non-contingent salary' argument may lack a statutory mechanism to become curable-by-disclosure, and instead just feeds into a prior, separate question of whether § 1090 is triggered at all — this is what actually moved my number up.

I continue to withhold full deference to the panel median because a real doctrinal fork remains open and, in my view, under-resolved even in the summary: whether an interest this attenuated (general operating budget, only ~40% city-funded, spouse's compensation not contingent on any single award, Harbor Light's continued operation and her continued employment not plausibly turning on this one RFP) clears the 'financial interest ... in the contract' threshold under § 1090 at all, versus being properly analyzed only under PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality (where recusal is the established, sufficient cure) — this is a threshold classification question logically prior to, and distinct from, the remote-interest curability question the panel focused on.

Okafor's own uncompensated board seat plausibly falls within a § 1091.5-type noninterest for noncompensated nonprofit liaisons/officers (consistent with 6/7 panelists), which would remove one prong of the problem but does not reach the spousal-salary interest, which remains the dispositive and more serious defect driving my probability.
authorities: Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests, incl. § 1091(b) enumerated categories — exact subsections need verification)
Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests, incl. noncompensated nonprofit-officer/liaison provision — exact subsection/text needs verification)
Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201
Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103 (PRA, cited for contrast on recusal-as-cure)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the spousal-employment interest even clears § 1090's "financial interest ... in the contract" threshold in the first place, given its attenuation (general-operating-budget funding, only ~40% government-derived, salary not contingent on any single award) — versus assuming that threshold is met and asking only whether an exception applies. The panel largely analyzed this as an exception-fit question, but if a court instead resolves it as a threshold non-interest question (more akin to PRA materiality), § 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule may never engage, and the contract could survive on a well-executed recusal record alone. I cannot confidently resolve which framing a reviewing court would adopt on these facts.
data.response_to_panel: The consideration that actually moved me was Theme F/Disagreement 2: the panel's fairly strong showing (4/7 explicitly, more implicitly) that the enumerated § 1091(b)/§ 1091.5 catalog has no category that squarely fits a spouse's salaried, non-officer employment with a contracting nonprofit. In round 1 I treated 'fixed, non-contract-contingent salary' as a live remote-interest escape hatch under something like § 1091(b); the panel discussion made me realize that even if that factual characterization is correct, it may not map onto any actual statutory remote-interest category, so it wouldn't make the interest curable-by-disclosure — it would just bear on whether the interest exists at all. That reallocation of where the doubt sits pushed my number up modestly. I did not fully adopt the panel median because Disagreement 3 (threshold 'is this even a § 1090 interest, or a PRA-materiality-only question') persuades me there remains a genuine, separate escape route the panel didn't fully resolve either, and I'm not willing to treat majority head-count on a contested doctrinal classification as itself evidence of the correct legal answer — Delphi convergence here reflects shared professional priors more than a resolved authority, so I moved only partway rather than to the median/mean.
